comparisons.title: {richestName}'s wealth is quite literally unimaginable.
We rarely see wealth inequality represented to scale. This is part of the reason people consistently underestimate the relative wealth of the super rich.
Every 10 pixels you scroll is {scrollRate}.
OK, we're coming up on the end now.
Lol, just kidding, we're only about ⅛ of the way. Keep scrolling though, there's more to see.
Let's put this wealth in perspective by comparing it to some familiar things.
Yearly income of the median American ( ~$63,180 )
How much Elon Musk makes while buckling his seatbelt (4 seconds, ~$63,420 )
The average cost of a house in the United States ( $405,300 )
How much Elon Musk makes in the time it takes to make a cup of instant coffee (1 minute, ~$951,000 )
All the money the median American will earn in their entire lifetime ( ~$2.5 million)
For the poorest half of humanity — 2.8 billion adults — average income is just $5,100 per year, or about $200,000 in a lifetime.
Even the fortunes of the wealthy and very rich people are dwarfed by the incomprehensible wealth of the 0.0001%.
Lifetime earnings of a US lawyer (on average ~$6 million )
Lifetime earnings of a US doctor (on average ~$6.5 million )
Estimated wealth of Ryan Gosling ( ~$70 million )
How much Elon Musk makes while watching a movie starring Ryan Gosling (Barbie, 114 minutes, ~$108 million )
We tend to put people like Ryan Gosling and Elon Musk in the same mental category: 'rich people.' But Ryan Gosling is about 12,000x closer to you than to Elon Musk.
Estimated wealth of Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon ( ~$400 million )
How much Elon Musk makes every night while he is sleeping (8 hours, ~$456 million )
Many millionaires may see themselves as fabulously rich, and often oppose policies aimed at reducing inequality.
But they have not fully grasped the enormous gulf between themselves and the super rich.
Elon Musk could've spent $1 billion every single day in 2025 and his wealth still would've grown by ~$370 million each day.

His wealth increased by ~$500 billion in 2025 alone, which equals the entire annual GDP of Denmark . In other words:
every product sold, every service rendered, everything the entire country of Denmark produced in a year — that's how much his wealth grew in one year.
While you've been scrolling through this, Elon Musk's wealth has increased by approximately $0.
{richestName}'s wealth is larger than the entire annual GDP of all but 22 countries .
If he had spent $1 million every single day since the birth of Jesus Christ, he'd still have ~$100 billion left. (assuming his wealth never grew further)
The average American would have to work for over 14 million years to earn this much money.
~300,000 years ago, the first humans ( Homo sapiens ) emerged in Africa.
So the average American would have to work ~50 times longer than our species has existed to earn Elon Musk's current wealth.
If you laid out Elon Musk's wealth in $100 bills, it would stretch to the moon and back ~1.7 times.
Ok, I think we've established by now how absurd this kind of wealth is.
But does that matter? Isn't this just how capitalism works?
Well, yes. And maybe that's a reason to change it. Consider this: Elon Musk's wealth isn't just sitting in a bank account.
At this scale, wealth isn't just money. It's power — over what gets built, who gets elected, and which stories get told.
Just one example: In 2022 Musk bought Twitter for $44 billion  (5% of his current wealth) and reshaped what billions of people see, read and believe.
We can have a world in which wealthy people exist, without handing nearly all money to the super rich.
No single human needs or deserves this much wealth.
But Elon Musk is not the only one with such an absurd level of wealth that it's really not comprehensible anymore.
The wealth of Elon Musk may seem staggering, but it is a drop in the ocean compared to the combined wealth of all ~{billionaireCount} billionaires on Earth: {allBillionairesFormatted}.

A trillion dollars is such a large figure that you might as well say "eleventy gajillion zillion dollars." So let's try to understand this by looking at what could be accomplished with fractions of this wealth.
As we proceed, try to keep in mind: all of this wealth is controlled by a group so small that they could fit in a single mid-sized concert hall.
That's the whole group. Every billionaire on Earth fits in there:
comparisons.imageAlt: Seating chart of a concert hall with capacity 3428

comparisons.title:
Before we start, a quick note:
The numbers below are estimates, based on the best publicly available sources I could find (linked throughout). Exact figures vary by methodology and change over time, but the goal here is to convey the order of magnitude—the scale of what could be achieved—not perfect precision.
Let's start small: What could we do with less than 1% of the money?
Eradicate Malaria
comparisons.description: Malaria is one of the worst infectious diseases ever visited on mankind. In the 20th century alone, malaria killed 150-300 million people. That's more than the Black Death.

comparisons.title: In 2024, there were an estimated 282 million cases of malaria and 610,000 deaths. Most of these deaths (95%) were in the African region, where 75% of the deaths were children under five. That's around 434,000 children.

All of these deaths are preventable. Treating and preventing malaria is a well understood science, universally practiced in the developed world.

It's estimated we could get on track to basically eradicate malaria globally by 2030 with an investment of ~$102 billion total. That would only be around 0.51% of the total wealth of all billionaires.

Around 1670 people will die from malaria today. A small group of absurdly rich people could stop it for a sum of money so small, that they would likely never even notice its absence. But they choose not to.
But Malaria is not the only infectious disease that we could treat or end for a fraction of billionaire wealth. So let's look at some other examples.
What could we do with less than 5% of the money?
End Tuberculosis
comparisons.description: Tuberculosis killed about 1.23 million people in 2024. It's the world's leading cause of death from infectious diseases, and among the top 10 causes of death globally. It would cost a total of ~$250 billion to meet the 2030 END TB goals.

comparisons.title: End HIV/AIDS
comparisons.description: HIV/AIDS killed about 630,000 people in 2024. Around 41M people are living with HIV/AIDS. It would cost a total of ~$143 billion to put the world on track to end AIDS as a public health threat by 2030.

comparisons.title: Eliminate Hepatitis B & C
comparisons.description: Hepatitis B & C causes about 1.3 million deaths annually and more than 300 million are infected. Hepatitis C is now curable; B is preventable by vaccine. It's estimated that ~$58.7 billion is needed to eliminate viral hepatitis as a public health threat by 2030.

comparisons.title: Combat Antimicrobial resistance (AMR)
comparisons.description:
Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the top global public health and development threats. It is estimated that bacterial AMR was directly responsible for 1.27 million deaths in 2019 and contributed to 4.95 million deaths.

Improving innovation and access to high quality treatment would cost about $ 315 billion until 2030, but would offer a 28:1 return on investment, while inaction would cost around $1-3.4 trillion per year by 2030.

comparisons.title: All of the Above — Combined
comparisons.description: Every cause listed above — ending malaria, tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS and Hepatitis B & C as public health threats, and funding antimicrobial resistance research and access until 2030 — could be funded simultaneously with less than 5% of all billionaire wealth.

comparisons.title: But infectious diseases are not the only global health challenges that are technically solved and we could address with more funding. So let's take a second and look at some other examples from the healthcare sector.
Eliminate Lead Poisoning
comparisons.description: UNICEF estimates it would cost a total of $1.6 billion to eliminate lead poisoning by 2040. In 2019, lead exposure was responsible for 5.54 million deaths and cost the world $6 trillion according to this study.
comparisons.deathLabel: Cardiovascular disease deaths due to lead exposure
comparisons.sourceName: The Lancet Journal of Planetary Health

comparisons.title: Even if you wouldn't care about the deaths caused by lead poisoning and only look at the money, every dollar invested in interventions returns hundreds or even thousands of dollars in benefits. Not doing this is absolute insanity, yet it's still a problem in many parts of the world.

Good job, you've already scrolled through 2% of all billionaire wealth. Keep in mind that every single pixel you scroll still represents $1,000.

There's a lot left to explore, so let's keep going.
End Preventable Blindness
comparisons.description: 1.1 billion people have vision impairment, including blindness, causing an annual productivity loss of ~$1.1 trillion. 90% of it is preventable or treatable through proven, cost-effective interventions like eyeglasses and basic surgery.

comparisons.title:
A total investment of just $7.1 billion from 2025-2030 would result in
- 198 million fewer people with near vision impairment
- 24 million fewer people with mild vision impairment
- 30 million fewer people with moderate to severe visual impairment
- 3 million fewer people with blindness.
Apart from the health benefits, this investment would return $28 for every $1 invested.
Universal Hearing Care
comparisons.description: 430 million people need hearing rehabilitation. Unaddressed hearing loss costs the world almost $1 trillion per year. Achieving 90% coverage by 2030 would cost about $238.8 billion total — and return $15 for every $1 invested.

comparisons.title: But curing health conditions is only half the battle when billions still lack basic needs and the infrastructure to stay healthy in the first place.
So let's take a look at what could we do with less than 10% of the money?
Clean Water & Sanitation for Everyone
comparisons.description: 2.1 billion people lack safe drinking water. 3.4 billion lack safely managed sanitation — including 354 million who practice open defecation. 1.7 billion lack basic hygiene services at home, including 611 million that don't have access to any facility. Every year, 1.4 million people die because of unsafe water and sanitation.
comparisons.deathLabel: deaths from unsafe water and poor sanitation

comparisons.title: Fixing this would cost about $114 billion per year and would save a life every 22 seconds. That's less than a third of what we spend on cosmetics every year. Every $1 invested would yield an average $6.80 in returns.
End World Hunger
comparisons.description: 673 million people experienced hunger in 2024. 1 in 4 people worldwide face moderate or severe food insecurity. This number increased by 42.6% between 2015 and 2024. Nearly half of all deaths among children under 5 are linked to undernutrition. $93 billion per year would end world hunger by 2030. The world spends ~7 times that amount on gambling every year.
comparisons.deathLabel: deaths of children under 5 are directly attributable to severe acute malnutrition (SAM)

comparisons.title: Let that sink in for a second. For less than 2.5% of all billionaires' wealth, we could completely end world hunger. Yet we let millions of people go hungry every day in a world that produces more than enough food for everyone.
But people don't just need food, they also need the means to prepare it properly.
Clean Cooking for All
comparisons.description: About 2 billion people cook over open fires or dirty fuels. Universal clean cooking by 2040 would cost about $60 billion total — and prevent 1.6 million premature deaths every year.
comparisons.deathLabel: premature deaths from household air pollution due to unsafe cooking methods

comparisons.title: And while we're on the topic of cooking, let's not forget that people also need electricity to power their stoves, refrigerators and other appliances that are essential for a healthy life.
Electricity for Everyone
comparisons.description: 730 million people still live without electricity. 80% of them in sub-Saharan Africa. About $230 billion total would close the gap by 2035.

comparisons.title: All health and basic needs combined:
comparisons.description: Every single thing I listed so far — ending infectious diseases, solving other health issues, providing clean water and sanitation, ensuring access to nutritious food, clean cooking, and electricity — could be funded simultaneously with less than 10% of all billionaire wealth.

comparisons.title: These interventions would prevent untold suffering, save millions of lives every year, completely transform the lives of billions of people, and create trillions of dollars in economic benefits.
And the truly crazy part? The world's billionaires could fund all of this and wouldn't even lose money.
Last year alone, their wealth grew by $4 trillion. So they could've funded all of the above for ~$2 trillion and still would've gotten richer by the same amount.
So far, we've been talking about survival. But people deserve more than just not dying. So let's talk about opportunity — the chance to build a life, not just survive.
What could we do with ~30% of the money?
Universal Education
comparisons.description: 273 million children are out of school. The financing gap to reach the SDG4 (Sustainable Development Goal: Education) is $97 billion per year — about $485 billion total to 2030. If we don't close it, UNESCO estimates the annual cost in lost human potential is $10 trillion per year.

comparisons.title: These children are not missing school because they don't want to attend, but because they don't have access to it. They are missing out on the opportunity to learn, grow and build a better future for themselves and their communities.
End Extreme Poverty
comparisons.description: 826 million people live on less than $3.00 a day. Reducing extreme poverty to just 1% would cost $318 billion per year. That may sound like a lot — but the world spends seven times that on alcohol every year.

comparisons.title: 1 in 10 people in the world live in extreme poverty. But since 1990, 1.5 billion people have been lifted out of extreme poverty. So we know how it works. We know the costs. The only question is whether we're willing to do it.
But millions of people aren't just poor — they're trapped.
End Forced Labor
comparisons.description: 27.6 million people live in forced labor today. A one-time investment of roughly $212 billion could end it — and would increase global GDP by $611 billion.

comparisons.title: But forced labor is not the only remaining issue. Even though the world pledged to end child labor by 2025, it's still a huge issue. 138 million children are still in child labor — including 54 million in hazardous work.

The most recent cost figures I could find estimated that an investment of $760 billion could end child labor, however this number is from 2004 and therefore quite outdated. That's why I didn't include it in my calculations. But given that child labor is closely linked to poverty, education and forced labor, we can assume that it would be significantly reduced by the interventions I already mentioned.
Education gives people opportunity. Ending poverty and exploitation gives them freedom. Now let's see what it would cost to provide a healthcare system so they have a chance to actually enjoy both.
Universal Healthcare (UHC)
comparisons.description: 4.6 billion people lack essential health services. Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to the full range of quality health services they need, when and where they need them, without financial hardship.

comparisons.title: It covers the full continuum of essential health services, from health promotion to prevention, treatment, rehabilitation and palliative care across the life course.
The cost of delivering complete universal health coverage (UHC) across all low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) is estimated at $2.5 trillion per year.
Everything I've listed so far combined:
comparisons.description: All of the causes I've listed so far — ending infectious diseases, solving other health issues, providing clean water and sanitation, ensuring access to nutritious food, clean cooking, electricity, universal education, ending extreme poverty and forced labor, and providing universal healthcare — could be funded simultaneously with less than 30% of all billionaire wealth.

comparisons.title: It means a child born in the future — anywhere on Earth — could grow up fed, educated, healthy, and free. Not in some distant utopia — in a few years, with money that already exists.
About $2.93 trillion of the $5.5 trillion total comes from ongoing yearly costs. But remember that the world's billionaires got richer by about $4 trillion last year alone.

If this trend continues, they could still fund all of this indefinitely and never run out of money. And some causes overlap, so the actual combined cost could be lower. But as I said in the beginning, it's not about the exact numbers anyway. It's about getting perspective on the scale of their absurd wealth and the problems we could solve with it.
What could we do with ~66% of the money?
We didn't even reach the 30% mark yet, so let's dare to dream even bigger and turn our attention to the planet we all share.
Because here's the thing: none of this matters in the long run if the planet itself is falling apart.
The climate crisis doesn't just add new problems, it makes every single one above worse. More hunger. More disease. More poverty. More displacement. More suffering.
It's one of the greatest threats humanity has ever faced. And somehow, we've decided we can't afford to deal with it properly while fewer than 3,500 people sit on more than $20 trillion.

The richest 0.1% of the world emit more carbon in a single day than someone from the poorest 50% emits in an entire year. Since 1990, the richest 0.1% have increased their share of global emissions by 32% — while the share of the poorest half of humanity actually fell.
Climate Adaptation for Developing Countries
comparisons.description: 3.6 billion people live in areas highly vulnerable to climate change. $365 billion per year is needed by 2035 — but current flows are only $26 billion. A 14x gap. $1.695 trillion would close this gap until 2030.

comparisons.title: The people least responsible for emissions are being asked to pay the highest price. 250,000 additional deaths per year are projected from 2030-2050 from undernutrition, malaria, diarrhea, and heat stress alone.

At the end of 2024, 83.4 million people were living in internal displacement — more than twice as many as just six years earlier. Many of them fled floods, droughts and storms made worse by a crisis they didn't cause.
Restore Degraded Land
comparisons.description: Achieving a land-degradation-neutral world by 2030 requires restoring approximately 1.5 billion hectares. Closing the funding gap costs about $300 billion per year. Every $1 invested yields $7 to $30 in benefits.

comparisons.title: Every time an ecosystem collapses, it takes species with it — species that took millions of years to evolve and will never come back. Scientists estimate the current extinction rate is 1,000 to 10,000 times higher than the natural rate.
Clean Up the Great Pacific Garbage Patch
comparisons.description: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch could be cleaned in 10 years for $7.5 billion. Ocean plastic costs the world $2.5 trillion per year in damage to economies, industries, and the environment.

comparisons.title: $7.5 billion. That's what it would cost to clean up the largest garbage patch in the ocean. The world's billionaires made that much in ~16 hours last year.
End Tropical Deforestation
comparisons.description: Stopping tropical deforestation by 2030 would cost about $2.6 trillion total. It would protect 85–170 gigatons of carbon, millions of species, and Indigenous communities whose land is being destroyed.

comparisons.title: Forests aren't just trees. They're the lungs, the pharmacy, and the water filter of the planet. When they burn, everything we've been trying to fix gets harder.
Close the Energy Transition Gap
comparisons.description: Developing countries didn't cause the climate crisis, but they can't afford upfront cost to switch to clean energy on their own. Closing their $1.43 trillion energy transition funding gap until 2030 would replace coal with solar and diesel with wind in the places where it matters most and is funded least.

comparisons.title: This wouldn't only solve the energy transition gap, it would also save millions of lives. Outdoor air pollution (overwhelmingly from burning fossil fuels) kills 4.2 million people every year. The energy transition we just funded also provides clean air.
And this would benefit everyone. Almost every person on Earth (99% of the global population) breathes air that doesn't meet WHO guidelines.

If you've made it this far, you've scrolled past the solutions to many major crises humanity faces. Disease. Hunger. Poverty. Exploitation. The climate. Every single one of them solvable. Every single one of them affordable.
ALL of the Above — Combined
comparisons.description: Every infectious disease I listed, lead poisoning, vision, hearing, water and sanitation, hunger, clean cooking, electricity, education, poverty, forced labor, child labor, universal healthcare, climate adaptation, land restoration, ocean cleanup, deforestation, and the energy transition — all of it, together.

comparisons.title: That includes the total cost of all interventions (some until 2030) plus the yearly costs of Universal Healthcare, ending extreme poverty and provide clean water and sanitation. It would take an additional $2.93 trillion yearly to fund these 3 causes further (~73% of what billionaires gained last year).

These programs combined would completely transform our world. Millions of lives saved. Billions rescued from poverty and disease. The planet's ecosystems restored. By inconveniencing just ~3,400 people, the entire human race could advance to a new, unprecedented level of development.
And all of them would still be billionaires afterwards.

A common objection is that their wealth is mostly in shares, so it only exists on paper. It's interesting that this argument only comes up when we talk about redistribution. When they want to buy Twitter for $44 billion, the money is suddenly very real.

But that's a topic for another time. The fact is:
their wealth is not just on paper. It's real and growing faster than they can spend it. And if we wanted to, we could redistribute it to solve major global problems and they would still be absurdly wealthy afterwards.

Instead, we're living in a system that redistributes wealth from the bottom to the top. For every dollar of new wealth in the world economy since 2000, 41 cents went to the richest 1%, and only 1 cent to the whole bottom half of humanity.

That's also the reason why developing countries can't fund the solutions to their problems themselves. They have a total debt of $11.4 trillion. That's less than the wealth of all billionaires, but they have to pay $1.4 trillion in interest payments for it every year.
That money could build schools, hospitals, and other essential infrastructure to improve people's lives. But it goes to interest payments to the richest people in the world instead.

This is not just about charity. It is about a system where countries have to borrow money at high interest to buy basic medicines — while the richest people on Earth accumulate wealth faster than anyone could possibly spend it.
Given the choice between millions of preventable deaths, and slightly reducing the fortunes of a few thousand ultra-wealthy people, how could anyone conclude that the deaths are preferable?
We cannot accept this level of inequality any longer.

So what can you do about it?

You're one person. You don't have $20 trillion. But this problem was never going to be solved by one person writing a check. It will be solved by millions of people deciding this is no longer acceptable.

Share this.

The most powerful thing you can do right now costs nothing: share this page. Most people have never seen wealth inequality shown to scale. That's part of why it persists. Every person who scrolls through this is one more person who can't unsee it.

Learn who's fighting this.

These organizations are working to close the gap between what the world could be and what it is:
  • Oxfam — campaigns for wealth taxes and economic justice
  • Tax Justice Network — tracks tax havens and advocates for fair taxation
  • GiveDirectly — sends money directly to the world's poorest people
Of course, there are many more. Learn about them and support them if you can. I'm not affiliated with any of these. They're just doing good work.

Vote like this matters.

Wealth concentration at this scale isn't natural, it's the result of policy choices. Tax rates, inheritance laws, corporate regulation, international trade rules. Every election is a chance to change who writes those rules.

Talk about it.

The biggest barrier to change isn't that people disagree. It's that most people have never thought about it at this scale. Bring it up. At dinner. At work. In class. The conversation itself is the beginning of change.

Go deeper.

Everything above was the moral case. But there are many more reasons to rethink extreme wealth, including psychological, political and physical ones. I reasoned through the physics from first principles here: Do We Need a Wealth Cap?

And most importantly: Don't let anyone tell you it's impossible to change this.

The only reason we're in this situation is that we accepted it. We can un-accept it. The money exists. The solutions exist. The only thing missing is the decision to act.

Wow, you're still going? Congrats, you now reached the point of their wealth that could fund all the things I listed above. Everything from this point on would still be theirs, if we don't collectively decide that the remaining money could be used for better things as well.
